title: Te Kaitiakitanga o Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku / Guardianship of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Technology
culturalContext: Students will explore their role as kaitiaki of Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku, the stream that flows through our college lands. This awa holds significance to Ngāti Kahungunu as a traditional food gathering site and source of wellbeing. The lesson connects to the pūrākau of Kahungunu's journey through this region, where he recognized the life-sustaining qualities of local waterways.
learningObjectives: Design and construct a water quality testing device using digital sensors to monitor pH, turbidity, and temperature levels in Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku
Analyze historical and current environmental data to identify changes in the stream's health and propose technology-based solutions
Create a digital storytelling project that documents the cultural significance of the awa and shares monitoring findings with our school community
Collaborate with local kaumātua to integrate mātauranga Māori with technological approaches to water conservation

activities.title: Whakawhanaungatanga ki Te Awa
activities.description: Visit Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku with kaumātua to hear pūrākau, learn traditional monitoring methods (observation of native species, water clarity assessment), and establish emotional connection to place through waiata and karakia

activities.title: Hangaia He Pūrere Aromatawai Wai
activities.description: Design and build water quality sensors using Arduino microcontrollers, waterproof cases, and calibrated sensors to measure key indicators of stream health, incorporating both scientific and cultural parameters

activities.title: He Kohinga Raraunga, He Kōrero
activities.description: Collect and analyze water quality data over two weeks, comparing findings with historical records from regional council databases and traditional knowledge shared by kaumātua

activities.title: Te Whakapuaki I Ngā Kitenga
activities.description: Create digital stories using Adobe Spark or similar tools that present findings, cultural significance, and proposed conservation actions to be shared at school hui and with local marae

resources: Hawke's Bay Regional Council water quality monitoring data and historical records
Local kaumātua from Ngāti Kahungunu with knowledge of Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku
Arduino starter kits with pH sensors, turbidity sensors, and temperature probes
Te Ara Encyclopedia entry 'Traditional Māori stories about waterways' and local pukapuka from Hastings District Library

tasks: Functional water quality testing device that accurately measures at least three parameters
Data analysis report comparing modern sensor data with traditional indicators and historical records
Digital storytelling presentation that effectively communicates both technical findings and cultural significance
Reflection on how technology can enhance kaitiakitanga responsibilities for Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku

nzcAlignment: Technological Practice: Planning for practice, brief development, outcome development and evaluation
Nature of Technology: Characteristics of technology, characteristics of technological outcomes
Technological Knowledge: Technological modelling, technological products
Key Competencies: Participating and contributing, relating to others
Science: Living World, Planet Earth and Beyond
